AI in Education.

A local statistics firm was interested in the public’s opinion on the future of AI in education. After taking a random sample of 100 people, they created a 95% confidence interval to estimate the proportion of adults who feel AI is the future of education. They conclude: “They are 95% confident that the population proportion of adults who believe that AI is the future of education lies between 0.489 and 0.682.”

Based on these results, we would like to test if the population proportion of adults who feel AI is the future of education is different from 0.5 (HA: p ≠ 0.5).

If a p-value was calculated using the same 100 people, would the p-value be smaller or larger than 0.05? Explain your reasoning.

Module 8 Video Assignment #2.

Below is a scatter plot and simple linear regression output from JMP that explore the relationship between highway mileage (hwy_mpg) and city mileage (city_mpg). Both types of gas mileage were measured in miles per gallon. The data used in this plot was from EPA testing of cars in 2021. The researcher, Juan Sanchez, is interested in predicting city mileage based on highway mileage.

a.) Interpret the slope (if appropriate).

b.) Suppose that a special type of car was built that got high highway mileage (60), but due to the stop and start nature of city driving only got 10 miles per gallon in the city. If this point were added to the data set and the researchers re-calculated the regression line with the updated data, how would the regression equation likely change?

Fall 2023:
Obesity & Fruit.
Suppose we are trying to predict percentage of residents who are obese using percentage of residents who ate fruit at least once per day. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

OR

Spring 2024:
Smoking & College Graduates.
Suppose we are trying to predict percentage of residents who are smokers using percentage of residents who are college graduates. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

OR

Fall 2024:
Math Scores and Eating Fruit.
Data was collected from the 50 US states in 2014. EighthGradeMath refers to the average score on the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) Mathematics test for 8th graders in the previous year (2013) and Fruit refers to the percentage of residents who eat fruit at least once per day.
Suppose we are trying to predict the average score on the NAEP Mathematics test for 8th graders using the percentage of residents who eat fruit at least once per day. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

OR

Spring 2025:
LEGO Prices and Number of Pieces.
LEGO sets are comprised of colored plastic interlocking bricks with accompanying gears, minifigures (LEGO people), and various other parts. We are interested in predicting the price of LEGO Star Wars themed sets posted on Amazon.com. For this study, 25 Star Wars LEGO sets were randomly selected from those produced between January 1, 2018 and September 11, 2020. Price refers to the cost, in dollars, of each set listed and Pieces refers to the individual piece count within each set.
Suppose we are trying to predict the price of a LEGO set using the number of pieces within the set. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

(ISU-5) Fall 2024:
Math Scores and Eating Fruit.

Data was collected from the 50 US states in 2014. EighthGradeMath refers to the average score on the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) Mathematics test for 8th graders in the previous year (2013) and Fruit refers to the percentage of residents who eat fruit at least once per day.

Suppose we are trying to predict the average score on the NAEP Mathematics test for 8th graders using the percentage of residents who eat fruit at least once per day. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

Suppose we are trying to predict the average score on the NAEP Mathematics test for 8th graders using the percentage of residents who eat fruit at least once per day. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

AI in Education.

A local statistics firm was interested in the public’s opinion on the future of AI in education. After taking a random sample of 100 people, they created a 95% confidence interval to estimate the proportion of adults who feel AI is the future of education. They conclude: “They are 95% confident that the population proportion of adults who believe that AI is the future of education lies between 0.489 and 0.682.”

Based on these results, we would like to test if the population proportion of adults who feel AI is the future of education is different from 0.5 (HA: p ≠ 0.5).

If a p-value was calculated using the same 100 people, would the p-value be smaller or larger than 0.05? Explain your reasoning.

Below is a scatter plot and simple linear regression output from JMP that explores the relationship between the square footage and sale price of houses for sale in Gainesville, Florida, in January 2024. a.) Interpret the slope (if appropriate).

b.) Suppose that a small house was built with only 1000 square feet, but it was built with all premium products and costs 1 million dollars. If this point were added to the data set and the researcher recalculated the regression line with the updated data, how would the regression equation likely change?

(ISU-7) Spring 2025:
LEGO Prices and Number of Pieces

LEGO sets are comprised of colored plastic interlocking bricks with accompanying gears, minifigures (LEGO people), and various other parts. We are interested in predicting the price of LEGO Star Wars themed sets posted on Amazon.com. For this study, 25 Star Wars LEGO sets were randomly selected from those produced between January 1, 2018 and September 11, 2020. Price refers to the cost, in dollars, of each set listed and Pieces refers to the individual piece count within each set.

Suppose we are trying to predict the price of a LEGO set using the number of pieces within the set. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

Used Car Sales.

Consider the following scenario and then answer the prompts that follow:

Population Information:
- The population is all used cars for sale in Iowa.
- The mean sale price of all used cars for sale in Iowa is $35,750.

Sampling Distribution:
- A sampling distribution was simulated by taking 10,000 random samples of size 10.

Random Sample A:
- One of the random samples of size 10 had a sample mean of $34,888. This is referred to as Sample A.

Bootstrap Distribution:
- A bootstrap distribution was simulated from Sample A with 10,000 bootstrap samples.

1. What is the approximate mean of the sampling distribution?

2. What is the approximate mean of the bootstrap distribution?

3. Are the means the same or different for the sampling distribution and bootstrap distribution? Explain why.

1. Explain why Shanae might decide to choose Bonus Program A

Fall 2023:
Obesity & Fruit.
Suppose we are trying to predict percentage of residents who are obese using percentage of residents who ate fruit at least once per day. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

OR

Spring 2024:
Smoking & College Graduates.
Suppose we are trying to predict percentage of residents who are smokers using percentage of residents who are college graduates. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

OR

Fall 2024:
Math Scores and Eating Fruit.
Data was collected from the 50 US states in 2014. EighthGradeMath refers to the average score on the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) Mathematics test for 8th graders in the previous year (2013) and Fruit refers to the percentage of residents who eat fruit at least once per day.
Suppose we are trying to predict the average score on the NAEP Mathematics test for 8th graders using the percentage of residents who eat fruit at least once per day. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

OR

Spring 2025:
LEGO Prices and Number of Pieces.
LEGO sets are comprised of colored plastic interlocking bricks with accompanying gears, minifigures (LEGO people), and various other parts. We are interested in predicting the price of LEGO Star Wars themed sets posted on Amazon.com. For this study, 25 Star Wars LEGO sets were randomly selected from those produced between January 1, 2018 and September 11, 2020. Price refers to the cost, in dollars, of each set listed and Pieces refers to the individual piece count within each set.
Suppose we are trying to predict the price of a LEGO set using the number of pieces within the set. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

Data was collected from the 50 US states in 2014. EighthGradeMath refers to the average score on the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) Mathematics test for 8th graders in the previous year (2013) and Fruit refers to the percentage of residents who eat fruit at least once per day.
Suppose we are trying to predict the average score on the NAEP Mathematics test for 8th graders using the percentage of residents who eat fruit at least once per day. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

OR

Spring 2025:
LEGO Prices and Number of Pieces.
LEGO sets are comprised of colored plastic interlocking bricks with accompanying gears, minifigures (LEGO people), and various other parts. We are interested in predicting the price of LEGO Star Wars themed sets posted on Amazon.com. For this study, 25 Star Wars LEGO sets were randomly selected from those produced between January 1, 2018 and September 11, 2020. Price refers to the cost, in dollars, of each set listed and Pieces refers to the individual piece count within each set.
Suppose we are trying to predict the price of a LEGO set using the number of pieces within the set. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

Bootstrap Distribution vs Sampling Distribution

The bootstrap distribution and the sampling distribution tend to have similar estimates of standard error, but the two distributions generally have different means. In a few sentences, please answer the following questions:

• WHY do the bootstrap distribution and the sampling distribution generally have DIFFERENT means?

• Can a bootstrap distribution and the sampling distribution ever have the SAME mean? HOW would that happen?

LEGO sets are comprised of colored plastic interlocking bricks with accompanying gears, minifigures (LEGO people), and various other parts. We are interested in predicting the price of LEGO Star Wars themed sets posted on Amazon.com. For this study, 25 Star Wars LEGO sets were randomly selected from those produced between January 1, 2018 and September 11, 2020. Price refers to the cost, in dollars, of each set listed and Pieces refers to the individual piece count within each set.
Suppose we are trying to predict the price of a LEGO set using the number of pieces within the set. Which variable would be considered the explanatory variable and which variable would be the response variable? Explain.

Bootstrap Distribution vs Sampling Distribution

The bootstrap distribution and the sampling distribution tend to have similar estimates of standard error, but the two distributions generally have different means. In a few sentences, please answer the following questions:

• WHY do the bootstrap distribution and the sampling distribution generally have DIFFERENT means?

• Can a bootstrap distribution and the sampling distribution ever have the SAME mean? HOW would that happen?

question:
Used Car Sales.

Consider the following scenario and then answer the prompts that follow:

Population Information:
- The population is all used cars for sale in Iowa.
- The mean sale price of all used cars for sale in Iowa is $35,750.

Sampling Distribution:
- A sampling distribution was simulated by taking 10,000 random samples of size 10.

Random Sample A:
- One of the random samples of size 10 had a sample mean of $34,888. This is referred to as Sample A.

Bootstrap Distribution:
- A bootstrap distribution was simulated from Sample A with 10,000 bootstrap samples.

1. What is the approximate mean of the sampling distribution?

2. What is the approximate mean of the bootstrap distribution?

3. Are the means the same or different for the sampling distribution and bootstrap distribution? Explain why.

I-STUDIO 2a — Good ear for music (statistical inference?)

Some people who have a good ear for music can identify the notes they hear when music is played. One method of note identification consists of a music teacher choosing one of seven notes (A, B, C, D, E, F, G) at random and playing it on the piano. The student is asked to name which note was played while standing in the room facing away from the piano so that he or she cannot see which note the teacher plays on the piano.

Suppose you want to determine whether a student named Carla has a “good ear for music” using this method of note identification.

a. Should statistical inference be used to determine whether Carla has a “good ear for music”? Explain why you should or should not use statistical inference in this scenario.

Next, explain how you would decide whether Carla has a good ear for music using this method of note identification.
(Be sure to give enough detail that a classmate could easily understand your approach, and how he or she would interpret the result in the context of the problem.)

I-STUDIO 4a — Walleye fisherman (statistical inference?)

Walleye is a popular type of freshwater fish native to Canada and the Northern United States. Walleye fishing takes much more than luck; better fishermen consistently catch larger fish using knowledge about proper bait, water currents, geographic features, feeding patterns of the fish, and more. Mark and his brother Dan went on a two-week fishing trip together to determine who the better Walleye fisherman is. Each brother had his own boat and similar equipment so they could each fish in different locations and move freely throughout the area. They recorded the length of each fish that was caught during the trip, in order to find out which one of them catches larger Walleye on average.

a. Should statistical inference be used to determine whether Mark or Dan is a better Walleye fisherman? Explain why statistical inference should or should not be used in this scenario.

AI in Education.

A local statistics firm was interested in the public’s opinion on the future of AI in education. After taking a random sample of 100 people, they created a 95% confidence interval to estimate the proportion of adults who feel AI is the future of education. They conclude: “They are 95% confident that the population proportion of adults who believe that AI is the future of education lies between 0.489 and 0.682.”

Based on these results, we would like to test if the population proportion of adults who feel AI is the future of education is different from 0.5 (HA: p ≠ 0.5).

If a p-value was calculated using the same 100 people, would the p-value be smaller or larger than 0.05? Explain your reasoning.
